tweets.title: 🚨 PoK intruder Rayees Khan (31) apprehended near LoC in Balakote sector 📍 Son of Mohammad Yaqoob Khan, resident of Taroti village, Tetrinote tehsil, Haveli district, PoK 📍 Crossed into Indian side of LoC before being detained by security forces 📍 Initial search report confirms no incriminating material recovered 📍 Currently under detailed interrogation to verify motive and intent 📍 Security agencies examining possible routes used for crossing 📍 Incident marked as third infiltration related apprehension in the sector this month 📍 Earlier two Pakistani nationals were also apprehended in separate LoC incidents during the same period 📍 Both earlier cases are under investigation by security agencies 📍 Heightened surveillance and alert maintained along Balakote stretch of LoC Media Report
tweets.content:
🚨 PoK intruder Rayees Khan (31) apprehended near LoC in Balakote sector

📍 Son of Mohammad Yaqoob Khan, resident of Taroti village, Tetrinote tehsil, Haveli district, PoK
📍 Crossed into Indian side of LoC before being detained by security forces
📍 Initial search report confirms no incriminating material recovered
📍 Currently under detailed interrogation to verify motive and intent
📍 Security agencies examining possible routes used for crossing

📍 Incident marked as third infiltration related apprehension in the sector this month
📍 Earlier two Pakistani nationals were also apprehended in separate LoC incidents during the same period
📍 Both earlier cases are under investigation by security agencies
📍 Heightened surveillance and alert maintained along Balakote stretch of LoC

tweets.title: About a month ago, I had the opportunity to interact with several innovators and hardware builders in Bengaluru. One thing became abundantly clear, we have the talent, we have the knowledge base, and we have the human capital needed to build world-class technology. Yes, we can debate the quality of education, research ecosystems, or policy support, but when it comes to sheer engineering capability and installed human capacity, India is not lacking. I personally know colleagues who walked into British, American, French, and Italian defence companies as if it were effortless. They have built careers at firms like Raytheon, Boeing, Leonardo, and L3Harris. If they are good enough to engineer some of the world's most advanced defence systems, they are certainly good enough for India's defence industry. The challenge isn't whether India has capable engineers. The challenge is whether we create enough opportunities and an ecosystem that allows them to build here instead of elsewhere.
tweets.content:
About a month ago, I had the opportunity to interact with several innovators and hardware builders in Bengaluru. One thing became abundantly clear, we have the talent, we have the knowledge base, and we have the human capital needed to build world-class technology.

Yes, we can debate the quality of education, research ecosystems, or policy support, but when it comes to sheer engineering capability and installed human capacity, India is not lacking.

I personally know colleagues who walked into British, American, French, and Italian defence companies as if it were effortless. They have built careers at firms like Raytheon, Boeing, Leonardo, and L3Harris. If they are good enough to engineer some of the world's most advanced defence systems, they are certainly good enough for India's defence industry.

The challenge isn't whether India has capable engineers. The challenge is whether we create enough opportunities and an ecosystem that allows them to build here instead of elsewhere.

tweets.title: Let’s understand the F404 to F414 Evolutionary Leap to understand technology change. GE’s transition from the F404 to the F414 relies heavily on 1. Blisk technology (which cuts out mechanical attachments to save weight and maximize airflow area) and 2. Powder Metallurgy (PM) for disks. 3 Further, The addition of refractory elements like Rhenium in Rene N5/N6 single-crystal turbine blades allowed the F414 to increase core temperatures significantly without melting the blade structure.
tweets.content:
Let’s understand the F404 to F414 Evolutionary Leap to understand technology change.

GE’s transition from the F404 to the F414 relies heavily on
1. Blisk technology (which cuts out mechanical attachments to save weight and maximize airflow area) and
2. Powder Metallurgy (PM) for disks.
3 Further, The addition of refractory elements like Rhenium in Rene N5/N6 single-crystal turbine blades allowed the F414 to increase core temperatures significantly without melting the blade structure.

tweets.title: Certain pro-import circles seem to have found a new phrase today: “the click jibe.” What they need to understand is that, by nature, many Indians dislike paying billions of dollars for imported weapon systems only to later read headlines saying those systems have been “cleared for export to India.” When you’ve paid in full, such language feels demeaning to people with self-respect. Also, when things get stuck after paying in full it hurts too.. be it system or LRU (engine). That is why criticism of excessive imports, and support for indigenous products even if imperfect in their early stages resonates with a much larger audience than many assume. So next time when you come up with that “click” jibe do some backup work 😅
tweets.content:
Certain pro-import circles seem to have found a new phrase today: “the click jibe.”

What they need to understand is that, by nature, many Indians dislike paying billions of dollars for imported weapon systems only to later read headlines saying those systems have been “cleared for export to India.” When you’ve paid in full, such language feels demeaning to people with self-respect. That is why criticism of excessive imports, and support for indigenous products even if imperfect in their early stages resonates with a much larger audience than many assume.

So next time when you come up with that “click” jibe do some backup work 😅
